steps.title:
Test Case ID: 100.1.1.1.1
Test Case Name: Inaccurate Offer Title
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the offer title is relevant and accurately represents the content of the offer.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Identify Offer Title: Locate and read the full offer title from the details page.
Analyze Relevance: Check if the title clearly reflects the app’s purpose, functionality, or content.
Detect Generic or Misleading Titles: Verify that the title is not overly generic (e.g., “App” or “Tool”) and does not mislead users about the app’s capabilities.
Compare with Offer Content: Ensure the title aligns with the app description and features.
Verify Compliance: Confirm that the title is specific, relevant, and professional.

Expected Result:
The offer title must be justifiably relevant to its content. If not, update the offer title and resubmit the offer.

Final Execution Report:

Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: The offer title “App” is non-compliant because it is too generic and does not reflect the app’s functionality. Suggested correction: “Contoso Task Manager.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1100.1.1.1
Test Case Name: App/Plugin in Title
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the offer title does not include the terms “app,” “plug-in,” or derivatives. The only acceptable reference is “add-in” for Office and SharePoint add-ins.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Identify Offer Title: Locate and read the full offer title from the details page.
Scan for Restricted Terms: Check if the title contains “app,” “plug-in,” or similar derivatives.
Validate Acceptable Term: Confirm that “add-in” is used if referencing Office and SharePoint add-ins.
Verify Compliance: Ensure the title uses correct terminology and does not include prohibited words.

Expected Result:
The offer title must not include “app” or “plug-in.” Only “add-in” is acceptable for Office and SharePoint add-ins.
Final Execution Report:

Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: The title “Contoso plugin” is non-compliant. Suggested correction: “Contoso add-in.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.1.1.1
Test Case Name: Common Noun - App Name
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the App Name is not a standalone common noun. It must include the developer’s name as a prefix or suffix to maintain uniqueness and professionalism.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Identify App Name: Locate and read the full App Name from the app details/listing page.
Check for Common Noun: Determine if the App Name is a generic/common noun (e.g., “Tasks,” “Notes”).
Verify Developer Branding: Check whether the App Name contains the developer’s name as a prefix or suffix (e.g., “Contoso Tasks” / “Tasks by Contoso”).
Refer Guidelines: Validate against Microsoft Teams Store naming guidance (no standalone common nouns without clear branding).


Expected Result:
The App Name must not be a standalone common noun and should reflect uniqueness and professionalism per naming guidance.

Updated Logic 
PASS:
The app name is not a noun, or
The app name is a noun and has the developer name added as a prefix/suffix.

WARNING:
The app name is not a noun but it does not include the developer name as a prefix/suffix.

FAIL:
The app name is only a noun  and does not have a developer name suffix or prefix.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL / WARNING]
Failure/Warning Reasons (If applicable):

FAIL Example: Tasks (common noun) with developer name suffix/prefix (per updated rule).
WARNING Example: BlueSky (not a noun) but no developer branding present.
PASS Examples:

BlueSky Orchestrator (not a noun)
Contoso Tasks (noun with developer prefix/suffix)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.1.1.11
Test Case Name: App Name Indicates Pre-Production
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the App Name does not include words like Beta, Dev, Preview, UAT, which indicate the app is not production-ready.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Identify App Name: Locate and read the full App Name from the details page.
Scan for Pre-Production Indicators: Look for words such as Beta, Dev, Preview, UAT, Test, etc.
Verify Compliance: Confirm that the App Name represents a production-ready app.
Refer Guidelines: Validate against Microsoft Teams Store naming guidelines.

Expected Result:
App Name must not indicate pre-production status. It should be updated to reflect a production-ready version.
Final Execution Report:

Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: The app name “Contoso Tasks Beta” is non-compliant because it indicates a pre-production version. Suggested correction: “Contoso Tasks.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.1.1.2
Test Case Name: App Name Includes Microsoft Product Names
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the App Name does not include Microsoft product names (e.g., Teams, Excel, Outlook) in a way that implies co-branding or official integration.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Identify App Name: Locate and read the full App Name from the details page.
Scan for Microsoft Product Names: Look for words like Teams, Excel, Outlook, Word, Microsoft 365.
Evaluate Branding: Determine if the usage falsely implies co-branding or co-selling.
Verify Compliance: Confirm that the App Name follows Microsoft Trademark and Brand Guidelines.

Expected Result:
App Name must not include Microsoft product names in a misleading way. References should be avoided or rephrased.
Final Execution Report:

Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: The app name “Contoso Teams” is non-compliant because it uses a Microsoft product name. Suggested correction: “Contoso Collaboration App.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.1.1.3
Test Case Name: Copy of an Already Published App
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the App Name is unique and does not copy the name of an app already listed in the teams store.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Identify App Name: Locate and read the full App Name from the details page.
Search for Duplicates: Check if the name matches any existing app in the Teams Store.
Verify Compliance: Confirm that the App Name is original and does not infringe on another developer’s branding.

Expected Result:
App Name must be unique and not duplicate any existing app name.
Final Execution Report:

Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: The app name “Contoso Tasks” is non-compliant because an app with the same name already exists. Suggested correction: “Contoso Task Manager.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.1.1.4 
Test Case Name: Profane or Derogatory Terms, Racially/Culturally Insensitive Language
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure the App Name does not contain profane, derogatory, or racially/culturally insensitive language.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Identify App Name: Locate and read the full App Name from the details page.
Scan for Prohibited Language: Check for profanity, slurs, derogatory terms, or culturally insensitive phrasing.
Assess Context Sensitivity: Consider whether any colloquial terms or slang could be interpreted as offensive or exclusionary.
Verify Compliance: Ensure the App Name is neutral, professional, and respectful to all audiences.

Expected Result:
The App Name must be free of any profane, derogatory, racially, or culturally insensitive language.
Final Execution Report:

Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: The app name “Contoso Badass Chat” is non-compliant due to the use of profanity. Suggested correction: “Contoso Chat.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.1.1.6
Test Case Name: The App Name Must Be Relevant to Users
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure the App Name is relevant to the app’s actual capabilities and understandable to users (e.g., naming aligns with bot, tab, or connector functionality).
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Identify App Name: Locate and read the full App Name from the details page.
Review Capabilities: Check the app’s actual capabilities (e.g., bot-only, tab, messaging extension, connector).
Evaluate Name Relevance: Verify that the App Name reflects what users will experience.

Example: If the app uses only a Teams bot capability, avoid names like “Contoso Connector.”


Verify Compliance: Confirm the name is descriptive and relevant to user expectations and usage context.
[Optional: Add App-Specific Context]: Include your app’s capability description to help reviewers evaluate relevance.

Expected Result:
App Name must be relevant to the app’s primary functionality and clearly understandable by users.
Final Execution Report:

Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: The app name “Contoso Connector” is non-compliant because the app is bot-only. Suggested correction: “Contoso Support Bot.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.1.1.7
Test Case Name: App Name Leads with Core Teams Features (Left Nav Shortening Issue)
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure the App Name does not lead with core Teams features (Chat, Contacts, Calendar, Calls, Files, Activity, Teams, Help), which can cause left-nav truncation to those feature names upon install.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Identify App Name: Locate and read the full App Name from the details page.
Check for Leading Core Feature Terms: Verify if the name starts with Chat, Contacts, Calendar, Calls, Files, Activity, Teams, or Help.
Simulate/Consider Left-Nav Behavior: Determine whether the installed app’s left-nav label might shorten to these feature names.
Verify Compliance: Confirm the App Name is structured to avoid leading with these terms (e.g., use “Contoso Chat Assistant” instead of “Chat Assistant”).

Expected Result:
The App Name must not begin with any core Teams feature terms to prevent left-nav truncation to Chat, Contacts, Calendar, Calls, Files, Activity, Teams, or Help.
Final Execution Report:

Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: The app name “Chat Insights by Contoso” is non-compliant because it leads with “Chat,” causing nav shortening. Suggested correction: “Contoso Insights for Chat.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.1.1.8
Test Case Name: “Microsoft” / “for Microsoft” / “for Microsoft Teams” / “for Teams” Used in App Name
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure the App Name is the product’s name without referencing Microsoft or Microsoft products unless the app has an official partnership. If referenced, the app name should come before any Microsoft mentions.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Identify App Name: Locate and read the full App Name from the details page.
Scan for Prohibited References: Look for “Microsoft”, “Teams”, “for Microsoft”, “for Microsoft Teams”, “for Teams” inside the App Name.
Evaluate Partnership Claims: If the name includes such references, verify if there’s an official partnership justification.
Validate Placement: Where a valid reference is necessary, ensure the App Name appears first, followed by context.

Example: “Contoso Connector for Microsoft Teams” (allowed only with official partnership).


Verify Compliance: Confirm that the App Name avoids implying co-branding or endorsement.

Expected Result:
The App Name should be the app’s own name and must not include Microsoft or Microsoft product references unless officially partnered, with the correct phrasing order.
Final Execution Report:

Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: The app name “Contoso Microsoft Teams Connector” is non-compliant due to direct Microsoft product reference implying co-branding. Suggested correction: “Contoso Connector.”

Verify Compliance: Confirm that the App Name avoids implying co-branding or endorsement.

Final Execution Report:

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.1.1.9
Test Case Name: Parenthesis Used in the App Name
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure the App Name does not use parentheses to include Microsoft product references or platform identifiers, maintaining a clean and standard naming format.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Identify App Name: Locate and read the full App Name from the details page.
Scan for Parentheses: Look for opening ( and closing ) symbols within the name.
Analyze Content Within Parentheses: Check if the text inside parentheses references Microsoft products (e.g., Teams, Microsoft 365, Excel) or platform descriptors (“for Microsoft Teams”).
Evaluate Syntax: Determine if parentheses act as a container for “for [Product]” or “[Product] version” phrases.

Example: “Contoso (for Microsoft Teams)” — FAIL.


Verify Compliance: Confirm that product references, if needed, use plain text phrasing (e.g., “Contoso for Microsoft Teams”) without parentheses.

Expected Result:
The App Name must not use parentheses to include, highlight, or separate Microsoft product names or platform identifiers. Any reference should be in plain text following “App Name for Product,” when applicable.
Final Execution Report:

Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: The app name “Contoso (for Microsoft Teams)” is non-compliant due to parentheses around the platform reference. Suggested correction: “Contoso for Microsoft Teams” or “Contoso for Teams.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.15.1
Test Case Name: Content Presented in All Capitals

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the app’s short description and long description do not contain text written entirely in ALL CAPS. All descriptive content must use standard sentence case or proper title casing as per Store guidelines.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Identify App Content : 
Read the short description and Long Description:
Refer the Long description and short description given in url 


Read for All‑Caps Content:
Check if any part of the short description is written fully in ALL CAPS.
Check if any section of the long description contains ALL CAPS phrases, sentences, or headings.


Verify Compliance:

Ensure both short and long descriptions follow standard capitalization guidelines and avoid all‑caps formatting except for allowed cases like acronyms (e.g., API, CRM).




Expected Result:

The short description and long description must not include all‑caps text.
Content should follow sentence case or Microsoft‑approved title casing.
Acronyms and product names in uppercase are acceptable (e.g., CRM, API).


Final Execution Report
Test Case Result:
[PASS / FAIL]
PASS

Neither short nor long description contains improper ALL CAPS content.

FAIL

Any portion of the short description OR long description contains all‑caps text (not including valid acronyms).


Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example:

Short description: “WELCOME TO CONTOSO APP” — non‑compliant; suggested correction: “Welcome to Contoso app.”
Long description: “THIS APPLICATION HELPS YOU…” — non‑compliant; suggested correction: rewrite using sentence case.
steps.details:

Test Case Name: Content Presented in All Capitals

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the app’s short description and long description do not contain text written entirely in ALL CAPS. All descriptive content must use standard sentence case or proper title casing as per Store guidelines.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Identify App Content : 
Read the short description and Long Description:
Refer the Long description and short description given in url 


Read for All‑Caps Content:
Check if any part of the short description is written fully in ALL CAPS.
Check if any section of the long description contains ALL CAPS phrases, sentences, or headings.


Verify Compliance:

Ensure both short and long descriptions follow standard capitalization guidelines and avoid all‑caps formatting except for allowed cases like acronyms (e.g., API, CRM).




Expected Result:

The short description and long description must not include all‑caps text.
Content should follow sentence case or Microsoft‑approved title casing.
Acronyms and product names in uppercase are acceptable (e.g., CRM, API).


Final Execution Report
Test Case Result:
[PASS / FAIL]
PASS

Neither short nor long description contains improper ALL CAPS content.

FAIL

Any portion of the short description OR long description contains all‑caps text (not including valid acronyms).


Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example:

Short description: “WELCOME TO CONTOSO APP” — non‑compliant; suggested correction: “Welcome to Contoso app.”
Long description: “THIS APPLICATION HELPS YOU…” — non‑compliant; suggested correction: rewrite using sentence case.

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.19.1
Test Case Name: Contact Details / Get Started / Help / Sign‑up Not Hyperlinked
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that Contact, Get Started, Help, Sign‑up, and email address references in the long description lead users to a working destination. In some cases, a link may appear as plain text in the description but still work—this should be treated as PASS.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:


Identify Long Description:
Refer the Long description given in url 

Locate References:
Look for any mention of:
Contact / Contact Us / Support / Email
Get Started / Getting Started
Help / Learn More
Sign‑up / Sign in / Register
or anyother links found.


Verify Link Behavior (Hyperlinked or Plain-Text URL):
For each reference found:

If it’s a hyperlink: click it; confirm hover shows a hand cursor; ensure it opens a valid destination (not 404/timeout/placeholder).
If it’s plain text (not visibly hyperlinked) but looks like a URL or email:

Copy‑paste into a browser (for URLs) or email client (for emails) and confirm it opens/launches correctly.
If it opens the correct page/client, treat as working.


Result Logic (apply exactly):
 
PASS: If it’s a clickable hyperlink and opens valid pages.
FAIL: A reference of plain‑text URL/email exists in app description, and when plain text URL/email copy pasted opens a valid page
The link does not work whether a plain text URL/email , when copy-pasted is not usable/invalid, or a clickable hyperlink is broken/404/placeholder
NA: No Contact / Help / Sign‑up / Get Started or any link references are present anywhere in the long description.

Expected Result:
At least one navigation/support reference in the long description must work (clickable OR plain‑text that successfully opens when used).
Final Execution Report:
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL / NA

NA Example:
No Contact/Help/Sign‑up/Get Started references appear in the long description.

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 100.1.3.3.1
Test Case Name: Competitor References

Objective:
Ensure the short description and the long description do not contain competitor references.

Execution Steps:

Identify short description and Long Description:
Refer the Long description and short description from given url.

Look for competitor names (e.g., Slack, Zoom, Google Workspace, Webex, Discord, Asana, Jira, Salesforce, etc.).
If any competitor is mentioned in a comparative or promotional way → FAIL.
If competitor name appears only for interoperability (e.g., “Import Slack data”) → PASS.


Expected Result:
No competitor references in either section, unless they describe functional interoperability.

Final Execution Report:
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL
PASS if:
No competitor references in short or long description, OR
Only interoperability mentions exist.

FAIL if:
Short or long description includes any comparison or unnecessary competitor reference
e.g., “Better than Slack”, “Cheaper than Zoom”, “More reliable than Google Workspace”.


Failure Reason (Example):
“Long description contains the phrase ‘Better than Slack’, which is a comparative competitor claim.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 100.1.3.3.2
Test Case Name: Offer Unsubstantiated Claim

Objective:
Ensure that the short description and the long description do not include unsubstantiated, exaggerated, or unverifiable claims.

Detailed Execution Steps (Short):


Identify Offer Description
Identify Long Description and short description:
Refer the Long description and short description from given in url 



Scan for Unsubstantiated Claims
Look for exaggerated or unverifiable statements such as:

“Best app in the world”
“Guaranteed to increase productivity by 200%”
“Fastest app ever created”
“#1 app for all users”
Any claim that sounds promotional, inflated, or impossible to verify.



Verify Compliance

Claims must be factual, neutral, and verifiable.
Descriptions must focus on features, capabilities, and value, not hype.




Expected Result:
The description must not include exaggerated, unverifiable, or promotional claims in either short or long description.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL
PASS

No unsubstantiated claims in the short or long description.
Statements are clear, factual, and verifiable.

FAIL

Any exaggerated or unverifiable claim appears in short or long description.
Examples:

“The fastest app ever created.”
“Guaranteed to double productivity instantly.”




Failure Reason (Example):
“Long description contains the claim ‘Best app in the world,’ which is unverifiable.”

Suggested Fix:
Replace hype language with factual value statements, e.g.:

From: “Best app ever.”
To: “Provides efficient task and workflow management features.”
